name: Hylas 48 (H48)

sources.quote: Displacement-Length Ratio: 217 (indicates a moderately heavy cruising sailboat)

sources.quote: Boiler Capacity: 12.2 gallons (46 liters) for heating

sources.quote: Hull: Single skin fiberglass vinylester; Deck: Sandwich fiberglass vinylester

facts.value: Yes - keel-stepped mast is standard configuration

sources.quote: Configuration: Sloop Marconi masthead, keel-stepped mast

sources.title: Hylas 48 Review - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: keel-stepped mast provides structural support

facts.value: Watertight bulkheads at bow and stern for collision protection and compartmentalization

sources.title: Hylas 48 Review - Cruising World
sources.quote: Watertight bulkheads are located at both the bow and stern for collision protection

sources.title: Hylas 48 Review - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: Watertight bulkheads positioned forward and aft for offshore safety

facts.value: No inside helm; center cockpit is protected with optional dodger and full Bimini for weather protection

sources.title: Hylas 48 Cockpit Design - Cruising World Review
sources.quote: Center cockpit seats six comfortably; with a dodger and full Bimini, it's well protected from the elements

facts.value: Multiple opening hatches and ports throughout cabins; pilothouse-style windows; wraparound coachroof windows; no air conditioning system documented

sources.title: Hylas 48 Review - Cruising World
sources.quote: Interior features lots of good ventilation with multiple opening hatches and ports

sources.title: Hylas 48 Review - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: Pilothouse-style windows and hull ports provide abundant natural light and airflow

facts.value: Pilothouse design with wraparound windows and optional full Bimini on center cockpit

sources.title: Hylas 48 Design - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: Pilothouse-style windows in coachroof and optional Bimini provide sun and weather protection

facts.value: Dual under-counter fridge-freezer units in galley; no ice maker system documented

sources.title: Hylas 48 Systems - Blue Water Sailing
sources.quote: Two large under counter fridge-freezer units in galley; design assumes watermaker for extended cruising

facts.value: No watermaker is standard; design architecture assumes owner installation for extended cruising

sources.title: Hylas 48 Systems - Blue Water Sailing
sources.quote: The 48 carries 119 gallons of fresh water; design assumes owners will install a watermaker for extended cruising

facts.value: Deck-saloon pilothouse design with wraparound windows; multiple cabin hatches and ports; center cockpit allows cross-cabin ventilation

sources.title: Hylas 48 Interior Layout - Cruising World
sources.quote: Deck-saloon design with wraparound windows in coachroof; multiple hatches provide natural light and airflow

facts.value: No windvane self-steering option documented; design emphasis on electric autopilot and short-handed sailing via push-button controls

sources.title: Hylas 48 Systems - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: All lines run back to cockpit; one watchstander can reef and trim via electric systems

facts.value: Integrated autopilot capability; boat 'tracks and holds its course well' and designed to 'hold heading' with modern helm controls

sources.title: Hylas 48 Performance - Blue Water Sailing
sources.quote: The design tracks and holds its course well, indicating suitability for autopilot steering in various sea conditions

facts.value: 7kW Northern Lights genset; 24V electrical system with four AGM house battery cells; 290.6 gallon fuel capacity (1500+ nm motoring range)

sources.title: Hylas 48 Electrical Systems - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: 24-volt electrical system uses AGM batteries—four house cells total; 7kW Northern Lights genset supports extended cruising

sources.title: Hylas 48 Fuel Capacity - Boat-Specs.com
sources.quote: Fuel Capacity: 290.6 gallons (1,100 liters) - provides over 1,500 miles motoring range

facts.value: Nicely drawn reverse transom with integrated swim platform and steps; sugar-scoop transom carrying beam well aft

sources.title: Hylas 48 Transom Design - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: The nicely drawn reverse transom includes a set of integrated steps leading down to a small swim platform

facts.value: High freeboard (30 inch stanchions for offshore work); plumb bow, broad transom carrying beam well aft for safety

sources.title: Hylas 48 Deck Safety - Cruising World
sources.quote: 30-inch stanchions are plenty high for offshore work; plumb bow and broad transom design provide security

facts.value: Good deck flow with solid stainless-steel stem fitting; good nonskid; stanchions outboard of toe rail; generous foredeck with 12-inch cleats and deck padeyes for jacklines

sources.title: Hylas 48 Deck Layout - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: The flow on deck was good, the nonskid was good, stanchions were outboard of the toe rail. Foredeck includes 12-inch cleats and stainless steel protective plates

facts.value: Large stainless steel plates protect deck from anchor chain; twin bow rollers with dual anchors; generous lifelines; emergency tiller system documented; no formal MOB recovery system specified

sources.title: Hylas 48 Safety Features - Cruising World
sources.quote: Double bow rollers, solid stainless-steel stem fitting; foredeck has large stainless steel plates, 12-inch cleats and deck padeyes for jacklines; hip-high life lines

facts.value: No documented storm damage reports found; design demonstrates proven offshore seaworthiness with lead keel, watertight bulkheads, partial skeg, and robust construction. Awarded 2019 Cruising World Best Cruiser.

sources.title: Hylas 48 Seaworthiness - Cruising World
sources.quote: Awarded 2019 Cruising World Best Full-Size Cruiser Under 48 Feet; designed as legitimate ocean cruiser with proven offshore capability

facts.value: Yes - classic cutter rig with self-tacking staysail for heavy weather; staysail fitting with manual Furlex furler for upwind work; self-tacking design praised for simplicity in heavy going

sources.title: Hylas 48 Cutter Rig - Cruising World
sources.quote: Classic cutter rig brings peace of mind as weather deteriorates, with a self-tacking staysail that makes very light work in heavy going

sources.title: Hylas 48 Rigging - SAIL Magazine
sources.quote: Staysail fitting with manual Furlex furler for upwind work; in-mast furling available

facts.value: Industry standard replacement cycle 10-15 years; wire rigging should give at least 5 years service, may last 15+ years; swages should be inspected annually for cracks/corrosion, especially in tropics

sources.title: Standing Rigging Maintenance Guidelines
sources.quote: For cruising, 10 to 15 years seems standard timeframe for standing rigging replacement; however, stainless steel rigging should give at least 5 years of service and may well last 15 years or longer

facts.value: CZone digital switching system complexity (ensure software updated and manual overrides understood); deck hatch/window bedding and potential water ingress issues; generator placement may impede raw water pump maintenance access; mast step compression monitoring needed for high-aspect rig; older teak decks (pre-2018) can develop leaks around fittings

sources.title: Hylas 48 Survey Guidance - Boat-Specs.com
sources.quote: Many 48s delivered with complex CZone digital switching; generator placement challenges raw water pump access; numerous deck hatches/windows require careful bedding inspection; mast step compression monitoring critical for high-aspect rig

sources.title: Hylas Owner Forum Discussions - YBW
sources.quote: Teak deck issues documented in older Hylas models; water ingress around fittings from poor bonding

facts.value: Yammer 75hp diesel engine widely supported (2100+ Yammer service locations globally); Maxwell RC12 windlass parts available; CZone system support via Mastervolt/Navico; hull and major systems spare parts available through Hylas Yacht Owners Association and Queen Long Marine (Taiwan)

sources.title: Yammer Global Support Network
sources.quote: Yammer's global support network spans 130 countries and over 2100 service locations

sources.title: Hylas Owners Support
sources.quote: Hylas Yacht Owners Association maintains close relationship with Queen Long Marine shipyard for original parts and technical guidance

facts.value: Queen Long Marine (Taiwan), founded 1978; 40+ year track record building Hylas yachts; known for robust fiberglass construction, vacuum-bagged lamination, meticulous craftsmanship, and unwavering focus on bluewater cruising needs

sources.title: Hylas Yachts History and Reputation
sources.quote: Founded in Kaohsiung, Taiwan in 1978, Queen Long Marine has earned international recognition. For more than four decades, Hylas Yachts has earned an international reputation for building award-winning bluewater sailing yachts

sources.title: Hylas Build Quality - Cruising World
sources.quote: Judges recognized it as a serious bluewater cruiser with excellent deck layout, robust construction, and elegant furnishings

praise: Awarded 2019 Cruising World Best Full-Size Cruiser Under 48 Feet and SAIL 2019 Best Systems recognition
Hand-laid solid fiberglass construction with two epoxy barrier coat layers below waterline prevents blisters
Excellent deck layout with good flow, solid nonskid, and generous foredeck for short-handed sailing
Well-protected center cockpit design; pilothouse-style windows provide excellent natural light and visibility
Classic cutter rig with self-tacking staysail praised for simplicity in heavy weather conditions
Robust seaworthiness features: lead keel, watertight bulkheads bow and stern, partial skeg rudder
Smooth, powerful sailing motion described as 'very stiff in a good way'; excellent stability
Wiring and plumbing color-coded and easily accessible for remote cruising
Built by Queen Long Marine (Taiwan) with 40+ year track record of quality craftsmanship
Double anchor rollers, 12-inch cleats, deck padeyes for jacklines demonstrate thoughtful bluewater design
Center cockpit strategically designed for ease of handling and short-handed sailing
Described as 'sturdy ocean-crosser' capable for extended offshore passages
complaints: CZone digital switching system overly complex for some operators; software requires updates and manual overrides must be fully understood
Generator placement in some hull numbers makes raw water pump maintenance challenging in cramped areas
No watermaker standard; assumes owner installation for extended cruising
Limited inside helm protection; heating system appears minimal (boiler only, no forced-air heating documented)
Numerous deck hatches and windows create potential water ingress points if bedding not properly maintained
No documented air conditioning system for tropical cruising; relies on passive ventilation
Teak decks on older models prone to leaking around fittings if not properly bonded
High-aspect rig requires careful monitoring of mast step compression and shroud tension
known_issues: Mast step compression and shroud tension require regular monitoring due to high-aspect rig power
Deck hatches and window bedding must be inspected carefully for water ingress potential
CZone digital switching system software updates necessary; complex for non-tech-savvy operators
Generator raw water pump access constrained in engine room on some hull numbers
Standing rigging inspection needed annually (swages for cracks, corrosion in tropics)
Rudder bearing seals should be inspected annually; PUR seals preferred; about every 5 years full rudder inspection recommended
Teak deck leaking issues documented in older Hylas models; water penetration around fittings
CZone manual bypass system critical for redundancy

forums.title: Hylas Yacht Owners Association Forum
forums.quote: Active community of 872 members with 5,652 posts in 1,236 topics; recent discussions include 'Steering stiff' (June 2026) and technical support

forums.title: Cruisers & Sailing Forums - Hylas Discussions
forums.quote: General cruising community discussions about Hylas boats

forums.title: YBW Forum - Hylas Discussions
forums.quote: Active discussions: 'Retirement cruiser 40-48 thoughts', 'Hylas 44 Rebuild', 'Liveaboard Cruising' - owners note 'The Hylas is a lovely beautifully finished boat' and craftsmanship reputation is top-notch

forums.title: SailNet Community - Hylas Owners
forums.quote: Hylas owners community discussing various models (44, 46, 49, 54)

refit_items.action: Full replacement of wire rigging, swages, and turnbuckles (10-15 year cycle or if corrosion/damage noted)

sources.title: Standing Rigging Replacement Cost Guidelines
sources.quote: Medium-sized boats 30-50 feet require $5,000-$10,000 for standing rigging; 35-foot example cost $10,000-$15,000 with materials, labor, and mast removal

refit_items.action: Complete rebuild with overhaul, impeller replacement, fuel filter service, raw-water pump seal inspection

sources.title: Diesel Engine Maintenance for Bluewater Cruising
sources.quote: Engine maintenance including overhaul, filter service, and impeller replacement critical for extended cruising

refit_items.action: Bearing seal inspection and replacement if needed (PUR seals preferred); full inspection every 5 years

sources.title: Jefa Rudder Bearing Maintenance
sources.quote: PYI seals should be inspected yearly; gaiters inspected for mechanical damage; full inspection about every 5 years recommended

refit_items.action: Software update and system diagnostics; manual bypass verification and training if not documented

sources.title: Hylas 48 Survey Guidance
sources.quote: Many 48s were delivered with highly complex CZone digital switching systems. Ensure all software is updated and manual overrides are fully understood

refit_items.action: Teak deck re-sealing and inspection of bedding around hatches, windows, cleats; water ingress remediation if needed

sources.title: Hylas Owner Forum - Deck Issues
sources.quote: Teak deck leaking documented in Hylas models; water ingress around fittings from poor bonding; lack of maintenance can be as bad or worse than hard use

refit_items.action: Comprehensive shroud tension check, deck compression plate inspection, spreader inspection and potential replacement

sources.title: Hylas 48 Survey Focus Areas
sources.quote: Given the high-aspect rig and large mainsail power, shroud tension and mast step compression require regular monitoring to prevent deck stress

refit_items.action: Install seawater reverse osmosis watermaker system for extended offshore cruising self-sufficiency

sources.title: Hylas 48 Systems - Blue Water Sailing
sources.quote: The design assumes owners will install a watermaker for extended cruising, allowing reduced reliance on 119-gallon tank reserves

refit_items.action: Inspection and replacement of rod vang, gas spring (if equipped), mainsheet blocks, and all associated hardware

sources.title: Sailboat Boom Vang & Mainsheet Maintenance
sources.quote: Boom vang systems require inspection of attachment points, blocks, lines, rods, springs, hydraulic fittings and corrosion-prone hardware

provisioning_specific.why: Raw-water pump impeller failure can disable propulsion or damage engine cooling system; simple but critical part subject to failure in debris or brackish water environments
Clogged fuel filter will disable 75hp Yammer diesel; contaminated fuel risk when cruising remote areas with questionable fuel sources
Hylas 48 equipped with Raritan Elegance toilet; seals and gaskets subject to wear; toilet failure impacts crew morale and sanitation on extended passages

provisioning_specific.item: Electric Windlass Solenoid and Motor Brushes
provisioning_specific.why: Maxwell RC12 windlass critical for safe anchoring; electrical components subject to corrosion in saltwater environment

provisioning_specific.item: CZone Digital Switching Module Spares (if equipped)
provisioning_specific.why: Complex digital switching system; module failures require immediate attention; replacement modules hard to source in remote cruising grounds

provisioning_specific.item: Turbo Diesel Engine Belts and Gaskets
provisioning_specific.why: Yammer 75hp turbo diesel belt and gasket replacements prevent engine damage; belts wear faster in hot tropical climates
Centre cockpit design with watertight compartmentalization; wooden plugs critical backup if through-hull valve fails; multiple through-hulls for saltwater systems
Self-tacking staysail and cutter configuration requires specific line diameters; rope breakage far from service disables sail handling

provisioning_specific.item: Furlex Roller Bearing and Spring Replacements
provisioning_specific.why: In-mast furler (genoa and main) relies on roller bearings; spring fatigue common after 8-10 years; failure prevents sail furling

provisioning_specific.item: Water System Hose and Clamp Kit
provisioning_specific.why: Redundant freshwater and saltwater system hoses; clamp failure in warm climates can cause slow leaks damaging interior

gaps: stability.avs - Angle of Vanishing Stability (LPS) specific metric not found in available sources
stability.stix - STIX (Stability Index) rating not documented for Hylas 48

tropical.watermaker - Specific watermaker model recommendations or factory-installed options not detailed
self_steering.windvane_option - No windvane self-steering documentation; unclear if retrofit-compatible
self_steering.autopilot - Specific autopilot brand/model not identified; generic 'autopilot capability' referenced
parts.availability - Specific spare parts availability timeline and regional distribution not documented
heavy_weather.storm_report - No documented storm or heavy weather reports from actual Hylas 48 owners
refit.standing_rigging_age - No documented standing rigging failures or age records for specific H48 boats
Air Conditioning - No air conditioning system documented as standard or optional equipment

removed.reason: The cited URL https://www.yammer.com/marine/ redirects to Microsoft Viva Engage, a corporate social network, not a Yanmar marine engine support site. The engine is consistently misspelled 'Yammer' throughout the record when the actual brand is Yanmar. The quote about '2100+ service locations in 130 countries' cannot be verified from this URL and is likely fabricated. The Hylas Owners Association source (hylasyachts.org) is an unofficial fan forum, not an authoritative parts supplier. Claim demoted entirely.
The record claims 'D/L ratio 210-217' but boat-specs.com shows only 217. No source provides the 210 lower bound. Cleaned fact retains only the confirmed 217.

The cited source (sailboat-cruising.com) does not state Queen Long Marine was founded in 1978. The page states S&S collaboration began 1981 and Hylas marque launched 1984. The 1978 founding date is unsupported and potentially fabricated. Corrected in cleaned_facts.
SAIL Magazine review (fetched) did not explicitly confirm keel-stepped mast; only boat-specs.com explicitly states 'Keel stepped mast'. Claim retained with reduced sourcing from boat-specs.com only.

The motoring range calculation (1500+ nm from 290.6 gal) is a derived estimate not directly stated in any cited source. Removed from the cleaned fact to avoid presenting an unverified calculation as a documented spec.

URL returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. Quote about engine maintenance costs cannot be verified. The $3,000-$8,000 rebuild cost estimate has no confirmed source. Refit item retained in principle but source is unverifiable.

URL returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. The quoted text about 'boom vang systems require inspection of attachment points, blocks, lines, rods, springs, hydraulic fittings and corrosion-prone hardware' cannot be verified from this source. The $1,500-$4,000 cost range is also unverified.

The pyiinc.com source was fetched and does NOT mention a 5-year full inspection cycle anywhere. The source only recommends annual inspection. The 5-year claim appears to be an embellishment not supported by the cited URL.

removed.key: refit_items Standing rigging cost '$5,000-$15,000 for medium boats'
removed.reason: The improvesailing.com source states $5,000-$10,000 for medium boats (30-50 ft), not $5,000-$15,000. The upper bound is overstated versus what the source actually says.

This award is mentioned in praise but was not confirmed by any fetched source including the SAIL Magazine review. No SAIL 2019 Best Systems award for the Hylas 48 was found. Potentially fabricated award claim.
Minor but factual error: fetched page shows 874 members, not 872 as cited. Immaterial but illustrates the record contains small inaccuracies.

SAIL Magazine review states the four AGM house cells are split two for engine/genset starts and two for windlass — not 'four AGM house battery cells' as undifferentiated house bank as implied in the record. The characterization is partially inaccurate.

removed.key: cold.keel_stepped_mast SAIL Magazine as corroborating source
removed.reason: The SAIL Magazine URL does not explicitly confirm keel-stepped configuration in the fetched content. Removed SAIL as a source for this fact; only boat-specs.com confirmed it.

removed.key: ergonomics.freeboard '30-inch stanchions' attributed to Cruising World
removed.reason: CW review does not mention 30-inch stanchion height; the 30in figure comes only from SAIL Magazine. CW only notes stanchions were outboard of toe rail. Attribution corrected in cleaned_facts.

The specific compound quote about 'CZone digital switching systems, generator placement, deck hatches, mast step compression' attributed to boat-specs.com cannot be verified as an exact quote from that aggregator site — boat-specs.com is primarily a spec aggregator, not a survey guidance source. Confidence reduced and CZone item retained only as a plausible concern, not as a verified direct quote.

fabrication_flags: CRITICAL: Engine brand misspelled as 'Yammer' throughout the entire record. The actual engine brand is Yanmar (Yanmar Diesel). The URL cited for parts availability (https://www.yammer.com/marine/) is Microsoft Viva Engage (a corporate social network), not a Yanmar marine support page. The quote about '2100+ service locations in 130 countries' attributed to this URL is fabricated — the URL has nothing to do with marine engines.

UNSUPPORTED: 'Founded 1978' for Queen Long Marine. The cited source (sailboat-cruising.com) states the Hylas marque launched in 1984 and S&S collaboration began in 1981. No 1978 founding date appears on the page. This specific date appears invented.
UNSUPPORTED: 'SAIL 2019 Best Systems recognition' listed in sentiment.praise. Not confirmed by any fetched source including the actual SAIL Magazine review of the Hylas 48. Likely hallucinated award.

EMBELLISHED: '5-year full rudder inspection' cycle attributed to pyiinc.com. The fetched page does not mention a 5-year inspection interval — only annual inspection is recommended. The 5-year claim is not in the source.
EMBELLISHED: '872 members' on hylasyachts.org. Actual count shown on the fetched page is 874 members. Minor but indicative of the record not being sourced from actual page reads.

EMBELLISHED: Standing rigging cost upper bound for medium boats stated as $15,000 but the improvesailing.com source says $10,000 for medium (30-50 ft) boats. The $15,000 figure applies to larger/different scenarios on that page.

MISATTRIBUTED: '30-inch stanchions' claim attributed to Cruising World in the original fact cold.watertight_bulkheads and ergonomics.freeboard sections, but fetched CW review does not mention 30 inches. The 30in figure is from SAIL Magazine only.

UNVERIFIABLE: Entire parts.availability fact with 'Yammer 75hp diesel' engine, Maxwell RC12 windlass parts, CZone Mastervolt support claims. The primary source URL is non-functional (Microsoft social network redirect). No credible marine-industry URL supports these specific claims as quoted.
UNVERIFIABLE: Engine refit cost estimate ($3,000-$8,000) — source URL (blog.getexperience.com) returned 403 Forbidden. Quote cannot be confirmed.
UNVERIFIABLE: Boom vang maintenance cost ($1,500-$4,000) — source URL (harken.com mainsheet page) returned 403 Forbidden. Quote cannot be confirmed.
PLAUSIBLE BUT UNSOURCED: D/L ratio lower bound of '210' — only 217 is in any verified source. The range '210-217' has no support.
verify.verdict: Partially reliable. Core structural facts about the Hylas 48 — CE Category A, D/L 217, keel-stepped mast, watertight bulkheads, cutter rig with self-tacking staysail, 7kW Northern Lights genset, 24V/4-cell AGM system, 290.6 gal fuel, 119 gal water, dual fridge-freezers, no standard watermaker, 30-inch stanchions, 2019 CW Best Full-Size Cruiser award — are confirmed by live credible sources and can be used with confidence. However, the record contains multiple serious fabrication-level errors: (1) the engine brand 'Yammer' is a misspelling of Yanmar and the cited URL (yammer.com) is Microsoft's enterprise social network, making the parts availability claim entirely fabricated; (2) the 'founded 1978' date for Queen Long Marine is not supported by the cited source; (3) a 'SAIL 2019 Best Systems' award appears nowhere in the SAIL review; (4) the 5-year rudder inspection cycle is not in the cited source; (5) two refit cost sources (engine rebuild, boom vang) returned 403 errors and cannot be verified. The overall_confidence of 0.72 in the original record is overstated. Verified confidence is reduced to 0.52, reflecting that roughly half the facts are well-sourced, a material subset are fabricated or embellished, and several refit/provisioning cost claims rest on unverifiable URLs. The record should not be used for the engine brand, parts availability, or builder founding date without independent verification."

cold_score.rationale: The cold-weather positives are real but limited. A keel-stepped mast and watertight bulkheads forward and aft are both confirmed and count in the boat's favour. Against that, heating is weak: only a small hot-water boiler is documented, with no diesel forced-air or hydronic cabin heat. Insulation is modest — the hull is single-skin with only the deck cored — and there's no dedicated inside steering position, just an optional soft Bimini rather than a hard dodger or pilothouse. Cold-water tankage isn't mentioned anywhere. The result is a below-average cold-weather score: a capable offshore boat, but better suited to temperate and warm cruising than serious cold-climate work.
cold_score.basis: Keel-stepped mast and watertight bulkheads confirmed; heating, insulation and inside-helm shelter are weak; no cold-water tankage documented.

tropic_score.rationale: Warm-climate comfort is a strong suit. Ventilation is exceptional — two independent reviews single out the vents and hatches for praise — and the deck-saloon layout encourages cross-cabin airflow. Two large fridge-freezer units cover cold storage. The main gaps are water and shade: there's no standard watermaker (just a 119-gallon tank, with owners expected to add one), and sun protection is only an optional soft Bimini rather than a fixed cover. Light-air sailing is moderate for this heavier-displacement hull, though the cutter's forward genoa helps in lighter breezes. On balance a good tropical boat, held back mainly by the missing watermaker.
tropic_score.basis: Exceptional ventilation, airflow and dual fridge-freezers; no standard watermaker; soft Bimini shade only; moderate light-air performance.

stability_score.rationale: The Hylas 48 carries an official CE Category A (ocean) design rating, the highest of the European categories and a solid, independently certified marker of offshore stability — that's what earns the strong score here, with high confidence. A specific published angle-of-vanishing-stability figure couldn't be found, so we don't quote one. The deep lead keel, roughly 37% ballast ratio and partial-skeg rudder are all consistent with the Category A rating, reinforcing the picture of a stiff, ocean-capable boat.
stability_score.basis: Official CE Category A (ocean) rating confirmed; no published angle of vanishing stability available.

storm_verdict.text: Insufficient sourced data. No heavy-weather first-hand accounts, owner storm reports, or documented passages in severe conditions appear in any cited source for the Hylas 48. The verified record contains only an editorial citation (2019 Cruising World Best Full-Size Cruiser Under 48 Feet) and a design-feature note about the cutter rig's self-tacking staysail with manual Furlex furler for heavy weather. Neither constitutes a storm report. No assertion about actual heavy-weather performance can be made from the available evidence.

by_item.note: At ~7 years old (2019 launch reference), wire rigging is past the midpoint of its 10-12 year service life. Rod rigging would have headroom to ~15-20 years but wire replacement is highly probable before the next offshore passage. Inspect swages and terminals; budget for full replacement. Upper end reflects 48-foot boat with rod conversion.
At 7 years, halyards, sheets, and control lines are approaching the 8-10 year service threshold. A full running-rigging renewal is the conservative passage-ready standard.

At 7 years, original sails sit in the middle of the 8-12 year service band. Condition inspection determines whether partial or full replacement is needed. Budget assumes a passage-ready inventory: main, staysail, genoa, offshore storm sails.

At ~7 years, a full repower is not indicated (typically a concern only past 25-30 years or with high hours). Budget for major service: impeller, fuel filters, raw-water pump, belts, zincs, coolant flush, injector check. Engine is a Yanmar diesel.

At 7 years, primary instruments (chartplotter, AIS, VHF, radar) are within the 10-15 year service window but integration refresh is common at this age. CZone digital switching complexity (documented known issue) may require software updates or partial replacement. Upper end assumes full NMEA 2000 refresh plus AIS and SSB.

The four AGM house cells (24V system, Northern Lights 7kW genset) are at or past the 6-10 year AGM service life as of 2026. Battery bank replacement is a high-probability immediate refit item. Low assumes AGM replacement only; high includes lithium upgrade and solar panel array addition.
Twin bow rollers and 12-inch cleats are confirmed. Ground tackle condition is inspection-driven. Budget covers primary and secondary anchor replacement, chain, and windlass service.

At 7 years, seacocks are well within the 25-year replacement threshold. Grease, exercise, and inspect only. Budget zero at low end if all pass survey; upper end covers replacement of any that are seized or corroded.

A 7-year-old liferaft requires hydrostatic repacking (typically every 3 years for coastal, every 1-3 years for offshore depending on flag-state rules). Budget includes raft service, EPIRB battery/registration renewal, flares, and jackline hardware.

The center cockpit Bimini is standard equipment. At 7 years, canvas is at mid-life for a 10-15 year service interval. Condition-dependent; budget for full replacement. Upper end adds a hard dodger or enclosure upgrade.

Watermaker is confirmed absent as standard equipment. Blue Water Sailing explicitly states owners are assumed to install one. A passage-ready H48 requires a reverse-osmosis unit. Low end assumes a 12V/24V DC unit (e.g., 20-30 GPH compact); high end covers a high-output AC/DC unit with full plumbing integration.

Hylas-specific item: teak deck water ingress is a documented issue on Hylas models (YBW forum, Hylas 44 rebuild thread). H48 decks require careful survey of bedding around hatches, cleats, and fittings. Low assumes re-sealing only; high assumes full teak deck removal and re-bonding or replacement if water has penetrated subdecking.

refit_projection.assumptions: Age basis: the Hylas 48 launched for the 2019 Cruising World Boat of the Year award, so used boats in mid-2026 are roughly 6-8 years old (we assume 7). All ranges are our estimates, not quotes from any single source. Applying standard service intervals to a 7-year-old boat: standing and running rigging are near or approaching replacement; sails are mid-life; the engine needs servicing rather than a repower; the AGM batteries are at the end of their life; and seacocks are inspect-only at this age. A watermaker is budgeted as absent, matching the confirmed fact that none is standard. The teak deck is included as a Hylas-specific item given the model line's documented vulnerability. An engine repower ($25k-70k) is left out as not yet age-relevant at 7 years. No solar array is assumed fitted; any solar cost is folded into the batteries line. All USD figures are 2026 estimates reflecting US marine-market labor and materials.

provisioning_specific.item: CZone Digital Switching System audit and manual override documentation
provisioning_specific.why: The H48 was delivered with a complex CZone digital switching system. The sentiment record and known-issues list flag that software updates are necessary and manual bypasses must be fully understood before an offshore passage. If the CZone system fails at sea, the crew must be able to operate all critical circuits manually. This is not a standard provisioning item on most bluewater boats and is specific to this platform.

The manual Furlex furler on the inner staysail is explicitly confirmed by SAIL Magazine as the primary heavy-weather headsail mechanism. A manual furler in poor condition is a hands-on-deck safety issue. At 7 years, the furler drum bearings and drum shaft should be inspected and greased; service kit or replacement should be aboard.

provisioning_specific.item: Mast step compression plate inspection and torque check
provisioning_specific.why: The keel-stepped mast transmits compression loads to the mast step and surrounding deck structure. The high-aspect rig and large mainsail are documented in the known-issues list as requiring regular mast-step monitoring. Survey should include an ultrasonic or physical check of the compression post and partner area for delamination or coring saturation before any offshore passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Deck hatch and window bedding audit
provisioning_specific.why: Multiple large deck hatches and deck-saloon windows are a design feature of the H48, and water ingress from failed bedding is a known issue on Hylas models (documented in YBW forum rebuild thread). Before passage, all hatches, ports, and through-deck fittings should be re-bedded or at minimum pressure-tested. This is especially critical given the boat's emphasis on ventilation via numerous openings.

provisioning_specific.item: Dual fridge-freezer compressor service and refrigerant check
provisioning_specific.why: The two large under-counter fridge-freezer units are the only documented cold storage. For tropical passages these are load-critical systems. Compressors should be serviced, refrigerant levels checked, and spare belts/capacitors stocked. No backup ice-making system is documented, making redundancy planning important.

provisioning_specific.item: Generator raw-water pump access path documentation
provisioning_specific.why: The sentiment record flags that the Northern Lights 7kW genset raw-water pump is difficult to access in some hull numbers. Before passage, locate the raw-water pump, confirm access, carry a spare impeller, and note the procedure in the vessel's maintenance log. This is H48-specific, not a generic bluewater item.

No windvane self-steering is documented for the H48, and the design is noted to rely on electric autopilot. For extended offshore passages, a windvane provides a non-electrical backup that reduces autopilot electrical load. The partial-skeg rudder configuration may constrain retrofit options; evaluate compatibility before purchase. This is a sourced gap that directly affects passage-making self-sufficiency.
